response:
Below is a complete pytest test suite following best practices, with full coverage for happy paths, edge cases, and error conditions. Tests are parametrized for maintainability and readability.

```python
import pytest
# Replace import below with the actual module where your function lives
from your_module import is_valid_email


class TestIsValidEmail:
    # ✅ Valid / Happy Path Test Cases
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("valid_email", [
        # Standard real-world use cases
        "simple.user@example.com",
        "user+mail_filter@example.co.uk",
        "user_name123@test-domain.org",
        "user%priority@example.io",
        "first-last.name@sub.domain.travel",
        "UPPERCASE.USER@EXAMPLE.COM",
        "x@y.dev",

        # Edge valid cases accepted by this implementation
        "a1234567890._%+-@example.com",  # All allowed special chars in local part
        "user@domain-with-numbers123.com",  # Numbers/hyphens in domain
        "very.long.tld@example.museum",  # Maximum 6 character TLD allowed by regex
    ])
    def test_valid_emails_return_true(self, valid_email):
        """Verify correctly formatted emails are accepted"""
        assert is_valid_email(valid_email) is True


    # ❌ Invalid Email Test Cases
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("invalid_email", [
        # Basic formatting errors
        "no-at-sign.example.com",
        "multiple@at@signs.com",
        "missing.domain@",
        "@missing.local.part.com",
        "missing.tld@example",

        # Forbidden characters
        "user name@example.com",  # Spaces
        "user!invalid@example.com",
        "user#invalid@example.com",
        "user@invalid domain.com",

        # Edge invalid cases
        ".leading.dot@example.com",
        "trailing.dot.@example.com",
        "user@.leading.dot.domain.com",
        "user@example.c",  # TLD shorter than 2 characters
        "",  # Empty input
        "   ",  # Whitespace only
        "\tuser@example.com",  # Leading whitespace
        "user@example.com\n",  # Trailing control characters
        "user@example..com",  # Double dot in domain
    ])
    def test_invalid_emails_return_false(self, invalid_email):
        """Verify malformed emails are correctly rejected"""
        assert is_valid_email(invalid_email) is False


    # 💥 Error / Bad Input Cases
    @pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_input", [
        None,
        12345,
        True,
        3.1415,
        ["user@example.com"],
        {"email": "user@example.com"},
        b"bytes@email.com",
    ])
    def test_non_string_input_raises_type_error(self, bad_input):
        """Verify non-string inputs correctly throw TypeError"""
        with pytest.raises(TypeError):
            is_valid_email(bad_input)
```

---

### Important Context:
This test suite matches the **exact behaviour of the regex you provided**. This is the most common practical industry email validator pattern, but note it is *not fully RFC 5322 compliant*. This implementation will incorrectly reject technically valid emails including:
1.  Unicode / international emails (`用户@例子.中国`)
2.  Modern gTLDs longer than 6 characters (`.engineering`, `.insurance` etc)
3.  RFC-valid quoted local parts
4.  IP address domain literals
5.  Single character local parts

You can extend the parametrized case lists easily as you discover edge cases for your use case.
